proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam description provides strong gameplay evidence: the game explicitly centers on turn-based battles, leveling up, gaining experience, growing bonds to unlock new abilities, traveling with a friend party, and using pair-specific Link Skills in combat. It also emphasizes an authored story about saving the world, explorable locations across two connected worlds, and environmental puzzles tied to world-switching. That combination strongly fits a story-forward JRPG structure with party progression and turn-based tactical encounters. Secondary CRPG/tactical signals exist from party management and puzzle-solving, but the strongest identity is JRPG-style story RPG rather than pure tactics or point-and-click adventure.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit on small-party authored JRPG structure, turn-based battles, character ability synergy, and frequent exploration/puzzle traversal between fights. Slightly less aligned on mystery/bond-social progression specifics.

Matches turn-based party RPG combat, teen ensemble, strong story focus, and substantial exploration with hidden treasure and puzzle-like traversal. More tactical/grid-leaning and less dual-world/mystery-driven than the anchor.

Good overlap on story-first indie RPG, party combat, emotional bonds, and movement between mundane and strange worlds. Less clear evidence for puzzle-heavy overworld structure and party-link mechanical customization.

Shares mystery campaign, close friend-group dynamics, turn-based battles, and relationship progression affecting combat. Its calendar/social-sim and dungeon routine is a more distinct structure than the anchor's exploration-puzzle JRPG flow.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present an action-RPG loop centered on choosing one of four hero classes, fighting hordes of enemies and dragon bosses, acquiring skill loot, optimizing builds through the Ancestral Grid, evolving character and gear, and continuing into endgame activities. The game also emphasizes up-to-4-player co-op across the full adventure and a hub-city upgrade layer, which fits a co-op-capable loot-driven action RPG more than any match-based or shooter taxonomy result suggested by the noisy computed near-misses. Evidence is strong for dominant combat, class/build differentiation, gear/skill progression, and replayable campaign structure, but insufficient to lock perspective or exact world topology more precisely.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: 4-player hack-and-slash ARPG with distinct classes, horde combat, loot progression, and campaign/endgame co-op structure.
Premier loot ARPG benchmark with class builds, co-op play, dungeon/boss farming, and extensive endgame progression; shared-world/PvP elements make it a bit broader than the anchor.
Fast co-op loot ARPG with strong class identity, massive enemy packs, gear chase, and repeatable endgame loops; excellent gameplay comp despite being an expansion listing.
Accessible class-based loot ARPG with co-op, randomized areas, and continuous build development, but lighter in endgame sophistication and overall structure.
Excellent match on buildcraft, skill specialization, dungeon crawling, and loot progression, but comparatively more solo/build-focused and less defined by 4-player campaign co-op identity.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify this as an 'action-packed, first-person retro shooter' set in the Starship Troopers universe, with the player joining the Mobile Infantry against the Arachnids. The Steam detailed copy adds key structure: a solo campaign 'packed with missions, secrets,' a large weapon arsenal, and 'epic battlefields,' which supports a campaign-driven first-person shooter identity rather than a match-based arena FPS. The Mobile Infantry framing, battlefield combat, and anti-Bug arsenal support a military-FPS classification despite the sci-fi setting.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on modern retro-FPS identity: fast first-person gunplay, chunky sci-fi arsenal, large combat spaces, secrets, and a linear solo campaign.
Excellent boomer-shooter match on aggressive movement, gore, heavy weapons, and hand-crafted campaign maps with hidden areas, though its broader mode set is less aligned.
Strong fit on high-action FPS combat, oversized arsenal, and mowing down huge swarms through campaign missions, but it is more arcade-horde focused than retro secret-driven.
Good match for classic solo FPS structure, key-hunting, secrets, and run-and-gun combat, but its Build-style exploratory level design is a bit more maze-forward than the anchor suggests.

A valid foundational retro FPS comparison for mission-map progression, weapon-driven combat, and secret discovery, but it is a broader classic benchmark than a particularly specific match for this anchor.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame the game as a comedy adventure built around 'tactical frog battles' where players 'collect, buy and trade frogs' to 'build an unbeatable roster' while embarking on quests and exploring an absurd world. That strongly supports a creature-collection RPG identity with tactical party-based battles, with tactical_rpg as a secondary read. Perspective, exact battle format, and setting are not clearly stated, so those fields remain conservative.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on creature collection, roster-building, exploration, quests, and turn-based battling; a strong audience fit even if its battle format appears more conventional monster RPG than explicit tactics.
Strong match on monster-taming campaign structure, squad progression, and strategic turn-based combat; less distinctive on adventure-comedy tone and not clearly as tactics-forward as the anchor.
Shares the core catch/build/battle adventure loop and open exploration, but it is a broader mainstream monster RPG comp rather than a close match for the anchor’s tactical battler identity.
Good overlap in collectible team-building and strategic monster combat, but the side-scrolling metroidvania structure and platforming make it notably looser than the other candidates.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Maestro as a VR orchestra conducting rhythm game. The core loop is timing- and gesture-driven: players use hand tracking to keep tempo, cue instruments, and shape music in real time, which strongly supports rhythm_game over weaker near-miss candidates. The game is described as playing through tracks/concert performances rather than exploration or narrative progression, fitting match_session structure, timing_input, rhythm_timing mechanics, and the hit_beats goal. It is single-player, non-combat, and presented as a premium replayable track-based experience with unlockable stages and cosmetics. While Steam also tags it as simulation/casual, that reads as secondary framing around realistic conducting technique rather than a different core genre.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Flagship VR rhythm game with motion-controlled, track-based precision play, high replay value, and similar mastery expectations; less expressive/conducting-focused, but still the clearest audience overlap.
Very close on VR rhythm identity, full-body timing input, song sessions, and performance flow. More note-lane/dance driven than Maestro, but strongly aligned on core loop.
VR music-performance rhythm game with physical instrument play, track progression, and skill mastery. The demon-smashing arcade campaign and drum-kit focus make it a looser fit than the top two.

Shares VR rhythm sessions, gesture timing, score chasing, and replayability, but its drumming-plus-racing structure and competitive multiplayer emphasis pull it away from Maestro's conducting identity.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently supports an online shared-world sandbox adventure with third-person exploration, building, terrain destruction, co-op/MMO-style social play, and some monster fighting. However, the core gameplay identity does not cleanly match any allowed archetype. It is not well-supported as mmo_action_rpg because progression structure, RPG build identity, and combat loop are underspecified; not open_world_action_adventure because construction/sandbox settlement play appears equally or more central than authored action-adventure structure; and not exploration_survival_adventure because survival pressures are absent. The current mapping-gap signals also show no candidate archetype reached threshold. Because the evidence is concrete but points to a gameplay blend outside the available taxonomy rather than a strong fit within it, rejection is more appropriate than forcing a mismatched archetype.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Closest overall benchmark: colorful voxel online sandbox with building, destructible worlds, exploration, multiplayer social play, and light action combat. Trove is more class/loot-driven, but the core audience overlap is obvious.

Very strong fit on third-person co-op sandbox adventuring, block building, island-based world structure, crafting, and real-time combat. Less MMO/shared-world than Altiros, but the play feel is close.
Strong match for persistent online voxel sandbox play, settlement building, world sculpting, exploration, and social progression. It is less combat-forward and more economy/city-focused than Altiros.

Major overlap in block building, terrain manipulation, exploration, and multiplayer sandbox expression. However, Minecraft is less specifically third-person, less MMO-shaped, and less anchored to action-adventure combat structure.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Pizza Slice as a restaurant business simulation centered on managing a family pizzeria: shopping for ingredients, prep work, making pizzas, expanding tables, buying better kitchenware, learning recipes, keeping customers happy, and growing from a small shop into a larger franchise. Metacritic explicitly calls it a strategy/simulation game with economic elements and a pizzeria manager role. Steam categories also support single-player, co-op, and PvP, with the detailed description naming an additive team-vs-team cook-off mode; however, the dominant identity remains the core business-management sandbox rather than competitive match play. The evidence strongly fits management_tycoon over city_builder/colony_sim because the focus is operating and optimizing a commercial venue and franchise growth, not urban planning or autonomous colony management.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct pizzeria economic sim with recipe creation, restaurant expansion, competitor pressure, and rise-from-small-shop-to-chain progression; this is the clearest gameplay match.
Very close on restaurant ownership, layout, menu design, recipe systems, staffing, and sandbox business growth, though it is broader restaurant tycoon rather than specifically pizza-focused.
Strong match on running a pizza shop, fulfilling orders, upgrading equipment and toppings, and staying profitable against rivals, but lighter and narrower in management scope.

Shares hospitality tycoon fundamentals like staff management, supply chain, customer satisfaction, recipes, and business expansion, but cafe simulation is a looser thematic and operational fit than a restaurant/pizzeria sim.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Mythmatch as a narrative-rich town/community-building merge game focused on rebuilding Ithaca, matching items and people, relationships, and replayable god-themed minigames. However, the evidence does not strongly support any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It is not clearly a city_builder, farming_sim, life_sim, management_tycoon, or puzzle-led archetype within the available list, and the referenced minigames appear additive rather than the core identity. Provider metadata also produces weak or noisy signals (for example co-op/multiplayer categories without concrete gameplay structure, and a simulation genre tag). Because the game’s core gameplay identity appears to sit outside the current archetype set, classification into an existing archetype would be speculative.

Stored evidence consistently describes a short first-person 'walking simulator with extra mechanics' focused on atmosphere, light fishing, and hide-and-seek/chase sequences with a revenge-seeking beaver. The horror framing is present, but there is not enough evidence for a supported core archetype such as survival_horror, psychological_horror, action_horror, or stealth_action_adventure: combat is not established, resource-survival structure is absent, puzzle structure is unclear, and stealth is only implied by hide-and-seek/chase wording. The available text points more strongly to a short first-person narrative/walking-sim experience, which is not an available taxonomy archetype here, so classification should be rejected rather than forced.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Best overall fit: first-person story-driven woods horror with exploration, light interaction, and survival through evasion/tension rather than combat focus.
Very close on first-person narrative-thriller/walking-sim structure and low-action tension, though it is more grounded mystery drama than chase-oriented creature horror.
Strong exploration/discovery overlap and no-combat first-person structure, but it plays more as a mystery investigation game than a pursuit-heavy horror vignette.
Shares the compact first-person narrative exploration core and wilderness setting, but lacks the anchor’s horror pressure and hide-and-seek/chase dynamics.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify the game as a 'mini metroidvania.' The gameplay copy supports that label with side-scrolling platforming, alternative paths, exploration across 4 biomes, dangerous creatures, powerful ancient items, boss fights, and a simplified experience system. Visual presentation is clearly 2D pixel art, and the game is single-player with a compact but authored campaign structure. While some combat specifics are not deeply detailed, the core exploration-gating-plus-bosses structure is strong enough to classify it as metroidvania.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close structural match: short 2D exploration-platforming with power-up gating, secrets, bosses, and a concise metroidvania campaign. More shooter-focused than the anchor, but scope and loop align very well.

A friendly, compact metroidvania with upgrade-gated exploration, secrets, bosses, and a breezier tone/challenge level that suits the anchor well. Slightly larger and more traversal-polished, but a strong audience fit.

Compact handcrafted metroidvania with map exploration, item unlocks, secrets, and bosses. The mech/cat split adds stronger ability-expression and combat identity, but it remains a very close genre and scope match.

Interconnected exploration-platforming with transformation-based gating and adjustable difficulty makes it a sensible comp. It is more classic action-adventure/platformer in feel than a modern mini-metroidvania, so the fit is a bit looser.

Clearly overlaps on side-scrolling interconnected exploration, ability gating, and bosses, but it leans more into tight combat, reflex challenges, and a harsher action profile than the anchor's lighter casual mini-metroidvania style.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently support a short, single-player, third-person, story-driven exploration adventure with light logic puzzles, item use, collectibles, and humor in a sci-fi facility setting. However, that evidence does not strongly map to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It is explicitly not puzzle-heavy, does not show clear platforming emphasis, does not establish meaningful combat loops, and lacks the stronger authored action/setpiece profile needed for cinematic_action_adventure or the horror/combat grounding needed for action_horror, survival_horror, or psychological_horror. This appears closer to a light narrative exploration adventure/walking-adventure hybrid, which is not an available archetype in the allowed list.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very strong match on short sci-fi facility exploration, low mechanical complexity, environmental storytelling, and narrative-first progression; weaker only because it is first-person and even less puzzle/item-driven.
Shares trapped-in-complex setup, authored progression, exploration, and puzzle-gated escape, but it is first-person and notably more puzzle-centric with a stronger bespoke mechanic.

Excellent fit on short self-contained story-led exploration with simple interactions, but the anthology structure, first-person presentation, and very different tone/world model make it a looser gameplay comp.

Third-person sci-fi exploration and light puzzle solving line up well, but it leans more into traversal/platforming and broader environmental journeying than Subsequence’s guided narrative-facility structure.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a soccer-focused game built around discrete matches: 'casual street soccer,' 3v3 and 4v4 play, and a 'Competitive Cup Mode' with tournament-style gameplay. The strongest supported identity signals are soccer as the sports theme, match-based session structure, and the goal of winning matches. Custom courts, ball designs, and floor styles read as cosmetic/environmental variation rather than a different genre. While perspective, presentation, and exact mode details are not specified, the gameplay loop is concrete enough to support sports_sim.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Best fit: explicitly street football, small-sided 3v3 play, stylized courts, arcade controls, and quick multiplayer-focused sessions align closely with the anchor's street-soccer match loop.

Arcade small-team soccer with fast matches and competitive multiplayer is a clear fit, though its item attacks, Hyper Strikes, and heavier combat-party flavor make it more exaggerated than the anchor.

Shares accessible arcade football, short match structure, tournaments, and local multiplayer appeal, but it reads more like a broad party-arcade soccer game than a specifically street-soccer playground experience.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a semi-open world puzzle adventure centered on combining magical runes to create spells, exploring interconnected areas, solving environmental puzzles with multiple solutions, and pursuing a branching mystery narrative. That evidence is strong for a systemic puzzle-adventure identity, but the available taxonomy archetypes do not cleanly cover this profile. The strongest supported traits are semi-open exploration, environmental puzzle solving, authored branching narrative, and systemic spell-based problem solving; combat is not described as a primary loop, and the evidence does not firmly establish a supported archetype such as open_world_action_adventure, western_narrative_rpg, or immersive_sim_action. Because the game's core gameplay identity falls between taxonomy options and forcing a combat- or RPG-led archetype would misclassify it, rejection is more appropriate than a weak accept.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Directly overlaps on rune-based spell crafting, combining magic effects, and using custom spells for environmental puzzles and traversal; more combat/boss-oriented and less branching-narrative-driven than the anchor.
Very close on exploration-led puzzle play, versatile abilities with many uses, and a large interconnected world gated by new powers; lighter on story, dialogue, and branching outcomes.

Strong overlap in mystery, exploration, dialogue choices, side-character involvement, and multiple endings, but its core loop is deduction and conversation rather than sandbox spell experimentation.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Parkour Labs as a movement-centric 3D parkour/platforming game built around speed, flow, precision, chaining techniques, discovering shortcuts, and unlocking new challenges across stylized surreal environments. The gameplay identity is strongly non-combat and mastery-driven, which aligns better with precision_platformer than action_platformer. Level/route unlocking and secret discovery provide a lighter secondary resemblance to 3d_collectathon, but the core loop is skillful traversal rather than collection. Multiplayer/PvP is supported by store metadata, though the main descriptive copy emphasizes single-player challenge runs and mastery. VR support introduces a first-person play option, while the broader labeling and non-VR presentation support a third-person 3D platformer identity overall.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Excellent fit: VR-native traversal/platforming focused on freedom of movement, level challenges, fastest-time play, and skill mastery. Very closely matches the anchor’s parkour/flow identity.
Very close on fast 3D parkour, momentum chaining, difficult level-based runs, and retrowave style. Combat tools and grappling are more central than in the anchor, but traversal still dominates.
Strong match on first-person speed-platforming, replayable challenge maps, and mastery-through-time-improvement. More aggressive and arcade-hardcore, with more overt attack mechanics than the anchor.
Good non-combat traversal comp with running, jumping, sliding, and climbing in first person. Much more peaceful, short, and pressure-free, so it only partially matches the anchor’s skill-test loop.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence clearly describes a single-player open-world space combat simulator with spaceship dogfights, trading, mining, mission-taking, ship customization, and exploration across star systems. However, the current Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not include a space-combat-sim / open-galaxy trader-pilot archetype, and the title does not cleanly fit any available accepted archetype better than a forced mismatch. Rejecting because the gameplay identity is strong but outside the supported archetype set.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close gameplay identity: single-player open-world space pilot sandbox with dogfights, trading, mercenary/pirate roles, missions, ship buying, and customization.
Strong overlap in single-player spaceship combat, exploration, mining, quests, and progression, but it is more fast-paced shooter/loot ARPG and less trade-sim sandbox than the anchor.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a fast-paced 'survivor-style action roguelite' built around quick replayable runs, endless monster waves, hero unlocks, random power-ups, and build synergies in fantasy arenas. Metacritic also labels it a third-person shooter, but the descriptive copy emphasizes a broader survivor/bullet-heaven style action loop rather than any supported taxonomy archetype here. The closest allowed archetype would be co_op_action_roguelite, but there is no evidence of co-op. Shooter archetypes such as arena_fps, military_fps, hero_shooter, loot_shooter, or third_person_cover_shooter are not well supported by the actual loop description. Because the game's apparent core identity does not cleanly map to an allowed archetype and forcing a fit would be misleading, rejection is appropriate.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The clearest benchmark for the anchor's survivors-like identity: short runs, endless hordes, meta-upgrades, character progression, and snowballing build synergies.
Very close on arena horde combat, hero selection, progression, and synergistic roguelite builds; more spectacle and boss emphasis, but still a highly natural comp.
Another strong fantasy horde-survival roguelite with repeated runs, hero variety, traits/items, and meta progression; slightly more Diablo-like presentation, but loop fit is excellent.
Shares fast runs, escalating enemy pressure, character variety, and build-making, but its short discrete waves and shop-between-rounds structure make it a looser match than the top three.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store text gives concrete gameplay, not just genre tags: it explicitly calls Anarchy Road a 'Post-Apocalyptic action driving game' where four heroes 'race against the clock,' 'battle bosses,' 'blast enemies,' and 'dodge traps.' Combined with racing/sports labels and co-op/split-screen categories, this supports an arcade-racer identity with strong vehicular combat and boss-focused setpiece encounters rather than a realistic racer or party game.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Best match for action-driving structure: vehicular combat, campaign progression, checkpoints/challenges, upgrades, co-op splitscreen, and explicit boss-like PvE adventure framing.
Very close on arcade combat-racing fundamentals: fast rounds, weapons, hazards, and local/online multiplayer. Less aligned on PvE progression and boss-focused encounters.

Strong car-combat racing fit with weapons, upgrades, campaign progression, and race/arena structure. More conventional competitive combat racer, with less co-op party/boss-run emphasis than the anchor.

Excellent pure combat-racing comparison on speed, weapons, split-screen, and track aggression, but it skews more hardcore competitive and track-mastery focused than Anarchy Road’s co-op action-drive adventure feel.

Good overlap in same-screen arcade vehicle combat, quick rounds, powerups, and solo support, but it reads more like a party battler/racer than a race-against-the-clock PvE boss-oriented action-driving game.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text strongly and repeatedly identify Solateria as an 'action Metroidvania' with hand-drawn side-scrolling exploration, hidden areas unlocked through skills, shortcuts, free-order world exploration, and boss-focused parry combat. staged_neighbors.why_similar: Closest fit: hand-drawn 2D metroidvania with heavy deflection/parry emphasis, tough boss duels, exploration gating, and a similarly combat-led progression loop.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Collector's Cove as a cozy, non-combat farming adventure centered on cultivating a floating farm, fishing, crafting, personalizing a ship-home, exploring islands, collecting entries for a compendium, and bonding with an animal companion. Strong cozy non-combat fit via collecting, fishing, crafting, customization, and relaxed progression. It leans more toward social sandbox/daily life sim than farming-adventure exploration.
Good match on tranquil fishing, collection, light crafting, boat upgrades, and low-pressure exploration. However, it is primarily a fishing/story/business loop rather than a farming sim.

Another near-direct annual-series match with the same core wrestling loop, realistic presentation, licensed roster play, Showcase content, sandbox modes, and custom creation depth.

Still very close in core identity: simulation-flavored WWE matches, Showcase, MyGM, MyFACTION, and roster-driven multiplayer, with only generational distance making it slightly less immediate than 2K24/25.

Combat is explicitly described as brawl-style beat-'em-up action with pausable party command decisions, leader switching, support combos, and coordinated attacks, supporting a western_narrative_rpg classification with beat_em_up as a secondary flavor rather than the primary identity. The mystery-driven quest structure, party management, authored campaign framing, and open-world exploration are all directly evidenced in the Steam detailed description and echoed by the short description/Metacritic copy.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Best overall fit: single-player companion-based action RPG with island exploration, quest-driven mystery/discovery, and multiple noncombat approaches. Combat is more conventional action RPG than brawler, but the exploration and party-RPG structure align well.

Strong match on party control, companion relationships, open-area questing, and real-time combat with tactical pause. It is more cinematic, larger-scale, and system-heavier than the anchor, with less of the brawl/investigation texture.

Good overlap in party management, island exploration, companion interactions, and authored questing, but its CRPG roots, camera/combat feel, and deeper tactical systems make it a looser gameplay match than the top two.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Cupiclaw as a 'cute & frantic claw machine roguelike deckbuilder' centered on grabbing prizes, building combos, customizing runs, upgrading the claw, and facing increasingly chaotic machines. That is enough to establish a clear gameplay identity, but the identity does not cleanly map to any allowed archetype in the taxonomy. It is not supported as sports_sim, realistic_racer, or pinball despite the current computed near-misses, and it also does not have sufficient evidence of card-battle combat to justify card_battler. Because the core loop appears to be an arcade claw-machine roguelike/deckbuilding hybrid outside the available archetype set, rejection is more appropriate than forcing a misleading fit.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: physics-driven arcade runs, drafting pieces into a machine-like board, and breaking scoring through chaotic synergies closely mirror Cupiclaw’s tactile combo-engine identity.
Excellent match on roguelite run structure, additive drafting, and machine-based payoff combos. Less tactile and physics-forward than Cupiclaw, but the slot-machine buildcraft is highly compatible.
Strong on escalating score engines, random-system exploitation, and run-based synergy construction, but its core play is poker-hand card optimization rather than arcade-machine manipulation.
Shares physics aiming, relic/orb synergies, and roguelite progression, yet its central loop is turn-based enemy combat, which is a meaningful step away from Cupiclaw’s machine-focused combo play.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store/metadata evidence consistently describes Bulb Boy 2 as a single-player point-and-click horror adventure built around solving tricky puzzles, navigating eerie handcrafted spaces, and unfolding a linear twisted story. Core signals are strong for cursor-driven interaction, environmental puzzle solving, authored linear progression, mystery/survival-escape framing, and essentially non-combat-first play. The text does mention facing jelly monsters and a boss, so I treated combat as light rather than none, but the dominant gameplay identity still aligns best with hidden_object_puzzle / point-and-click puzzle adventure. Horror tone is strong but not enough to override the puzzle-first structure as the primary archetype.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct predecessor in the same series with essentially the same 2D point-and-click horror adventure loop, puzzle gating, linear progression, and audience expectation.
Very close on single-player point-and-click structure, dark storybook horror, exploration, and puzzle-gated progression, with only some added minigame/music elements differentiating it.
Strong match on creepy 2D point-and-click puzzle adventure and story-led progression, though it is more psychologically focused and somewhat more narrative/feature-heavy than the anchor.
Fits the dark point-and-click puzzle space well, but its action-reaction design, nonlinearity, and replay emphasis make it less structurally similar to the anchor's more authored linear flow.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on side-view action-platforming, wrench/tool identity, gated routes, big-world progression, and boss-heavy campaign flow.
Excellent structural fit as a 2D metroidvania with traversal/tool upgrades, backtracking, and underworld exploration, though mining/economy is more central here.

Colony_sim and city_builder are nearby matches because of survivor assignment, construction, and base defense, but the clearest central fantasy is operating and optimizing a business in an apocalypse.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very strong match on shop layout, stocking, pricing, crafting, expansion, and customer-service loops. It lacks the zombie defense/combat layer, but it best matches the anchor's merchant-management identity.

Good metroidvania comp for side-scrolling exploration, ability gating, bosses, and procedurally assembled maps, but it is more traditional and single-player, with less roguelite repetition and no village/co-op layer.
Reasonable neighbor through side-scrolling roguelite runs, persistent upgrades, and home-base growth, but it is more run/stage-driven than exploration-led metroidvania and lacks co-op.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Rotwood as a 1-4 player hack-and-slash roguelike/dungeon crawler built around repeat runs, co-op combat, boss fights, weapon mastery, gear upgrading, and bringing spoils back to craft equipment and improve a safe haven. Metacritic also labels it a '2D Beat-'Em-Up,' which supports the side-scrolling/beat-em-up secondary read, but the stronger identity is cooperative action roguelite due to run-based structure, repeatable progression, and meta/base advancement.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored DLC descriptions consistently describe a combat-centric action game built around three new campaign chapters, unique levels, new weapons, bosses, fiend enemies, deadly trials, and an endurance-style challenge mode with successive combat encounters. proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently portray Aquamarine as a single-player, turn-based sci-fi survival/exploration adventure focused on foraging, cultivation, salvage, loadout upgrades, navigational challenges, and environmental puzzle solving in an alien ocean. It is not well-supported as hidden_object_puzzle because the evidence emphasizes survival, navigation, and environmental problem solving rather than hidden-object scenes. It also does not cleanly map to exploration_survival_adventure because the available archetype implies a more standard real-time survival-adventure identity than the game's turn-based, comic-book, puzzle-forward structure. Because the evidence is solid about what the game is, but does not strongly support any allowed archetype, rejection is the safest taxonomy outcome.

Strong puzzle pedigree with falling-piece pressure and space optimization, but tetromino line-clearing is a different core loop and the package leans more heavily into mode variety and multiplayer.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Royal Vermin as a 2D local platform fighter for 2-4 players, explicitly 'inspired by the best platform fighters.' The core loop is match-based PvP: players climb, throw, use ranged chargeable projectiles, knock opponents out of collapsing arenas, and aim to win rounds in fast, chaotic matches. Side-scrolling presentation, duel-focused combat, dominant combat presence, and win-match goals strongly support traditional_fighter over platformer; platforming is present but serves the fighting format rather than precision challenge progression. Party-game qualities are notable through local same-screen multiplayer and chaotic comeback-focused design, but they are secondary to the fighter identity.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The clearest genre benchmark: side-view platform-fighter matches built on launch/ring-out rules, stage hazards, items, multiplayer free-for-alls, and broad party accessibility.
Very close fit as a free-to-play 2D platform fighter with accessible controls, local/online multiplayer, item and projectile interaction, and chaotic knockout-driven rounds.

A highly relevant platform-fighter comp with the same side-on arena combat, aerial movement, edge pressure, and multiplayer versus flow, though it skews more competitive and mechanics-focused than Royal Vermin.

Still a solid platform-brawler match with local-versus play, character movesets, launches, and party-friendly multiplayer, but its presentation around supers and broader campaign framing makes it a slightly looser identity fit.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Ella Stars as an 'action-rhythm adventure' built around 30 original songs, with explicit rhythm gameplay, epic/intense boss battles, perfect combo finishers, adjustable difficulty, and single-player play. That strongly supports rhythm_game as the core identity, with timing-based play and boss-focused encounters as the main loop. Evidence for exact camera/topology is not explicit, so those fields are left empty rather than inferred.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on rhythm-synced action, authored progression, stylish combat, and major boss encounters; it is larger-scale 3D character action, so not quite the same gameplay identity.
Strong fit through beat-timed action, stage-based progression, repeated challenge play, and boss fights, though its core loop leans more platformer than melee boss-action adventure.
Good match on song-by-song rhythm encounters and challenge-driven single-player structure, but its battles are more pattern-dodging rhythm sequences than Ella Stars' action-melee boss combat.

Shares music-driven action-adventure structure and memorable boss battles, but combat is less strictly rhythm-timing dependent and plays more like broader action-adventure than a true rhythm-action game.

title: Welcome to Doll Town

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a single-player, third-person horror experience centered on exploration, mystery-solving, eerie puzzles, and escape rather than combat. Key phrases include 'Third-Person Exploration,' 'solve eerie puzzles,' 'uncover the truth,' 'slow-burn descent into horror,' and reality/dream dissolution, which strongly align with psychological horror. The cursed village, haunted dollhouses, environmental storytelling, and emphasis on dread and mystery support a narrative-driven psychological horror classification more strongly than action_horror or survival_horror, both of which lack concrete combat evidence here.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on third-person psychological horror, investigation, environmental storytelling, puzzle-solving, and limited direct combat emphasis.
Strong match on third-person town exploration, puzzles, oppressive atmosphere, and psychological mystery, though it has more overt combat and survival-horror structure.
Good fit for exploration, puzzle-solving, evasion, and tense horror progression, but its fixed-camera style and stronger survival-horror fail-state systems make it less like the anchor.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Fatal Frame: Maiden of the Black Water
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Fatal Frame: Maiden of Black Water
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Shares Japanese supernatural horror, cursed locations, mystery-driven exploration, and even doll imagery, but camera-based combat is a much more central gameplay pillar.

missing_must_include_titles: Fatal Frame: Maiden of Black Water

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies SealChain as a single-player roguelike centered on item-linking, formations/seals, random events, character builds, talent trees, and combat against monsters. However, the available text does not establish a strong fit to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It is not clearly a card battler despite combinatorial item synergies, not clearly turn-based tactics despite strategy language, and not supportable as co_op_action_roguelite because the evidence indicates single-player only. The Metacritic 'Top-Down Shoot-'Em-Up' label is too weak and isolated to anchor a shooter archetype. Overall the gameplay identity is real but falls between the available archetypes, so classification should be rejected rather than forced.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Best overall fit: single-player action roguelike runs with monster survival plus inventory organization as a core progression puzzle. It lacks SealChain's exact linking/seal formation system, but the spatial optimization layer is a very close mechanical neighbor.

Very strong match on run-based item placement, adjacency-style synergies, and build definition through bag layout. Ranked below God of Weapons because its combat structure is more turn-based/deckbuilding-oriented and less evidently aligned with SealChain's action framing.

Strong benchmark for top-down roguelike runs, monster combat, randomization, and wild item-combo mutation. It is less precise because the core differentiation in SealChain seems to be spatial linking/formation optimization, which Isaac does not center.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Stillborn Slayer as a single-player dark fantasy action roguelike with fast hack-and-slash combat, procedural runs, brutal difficulty, boss fights, dodging/rolling, and a grim horror-inflected tone. However, the evidence does not firmly establish a supported taxonomy archetype from the allowed list. The strongest identity is run-based action roguelite, but the available archetype list only includes co_op_action_roguelite, which contradicts the explicitly single-player framing. Alternative fits such as soulslike_action_rpg or character_action remain too weak because perspective is not established, progression is described as run/repetition-based rather than RPG progression, and the copy also emphasizes bullet-hell roguelike structure over a stable campaign RPG format. Because the title's core gameplay identity appears to fall between unsupported/insufficiently evidenced archetypes, classification should be rejected for now.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit on dark fantasy action-roguelite structure, fast melee combat, repeated runs, boss progression, and skill-based survival in a bleak world.
Excellent gameplay match: punishing run-based action, dodge timing, melee emphasis, traps, bosses, and procedural replayability with minimal story distraction.
A core benchmark for fast action-roguelike runs, enemy-pattern mastery, bosses, and build variation, though it is more narrative-driven and meta-progression heavy.
Shares action-roguelite runs, skilled dodge-centric combat, bosses, and bleak mood, but the waking-life/social layer makes its structure less purely combat-run focused.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame the game as a 30+ hour JRPG with a party-based adventure, explorable isometric dungeons, a world map linking towns and quests, and explicit turn-based battles where the player chooses each party member's actions and plans turns tactically. The text also emphasizes non-linear progression after the prologue, several branching endings, relationship/trust systems with party members and towns, and equipment/resource loops for commissioning gear. That combination strongly supports jrpg_story_rpg as the core identity, with some tactical overlap from its turn-based party combat.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close match on story-driven party JRPG structure, continent exploration, dungeon crawling, turn-based battles, and gear/crafting progression. Less aligned on town-trust and supply systems, but the core play loop is highly compatible.

Strong fit for overworld-to-dungeon JRPG flow, turn-based party combat, rebellion/war narrative framing, ally recruitment, and base-building progression. Broader roster focus makes it a bit less tightly matched than the top pick.

Good comparison for retro-style, story-driven party RPG combat and anti-regime political campaign structure. Feels more linear and less systems-rich around exploration, town development, and resource loops than the anchor.

Shares turn-based JRPG combat, party growth, town interaction, and open destination choice. The eight-protagonist chapter format and distinct path-action structure make its campaign identity looser than the anchor’s unified party adventure.

removed_candidates: Atelier Sophie 2: The Alchemist & The Mysterious Dream

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored publisher/store text consistently describes Ys X: Proud Nordics as the definitive edition of an action RPG adventure centered on fast-paced combat, exploration of a large island/map area, boss fights, and an authored story campaign. The copy emphasizes 'fast-paced combat,' 'Cross Action,' 'Mana Action,' new bosses, a high-difficulty dungeon, and broad map/exploration content, all of which support a single-player fantasy action-RPG identity. The current computed taxonomy pointing to traditional_fighter is contradicted by the actual descriptive text, which clearly frames a narrative-driven exploratory action RPG rather than a PvP or match-based fighter. The strongest fit in the available taxonomy is open_world_fantasy_action_rpg, though the world structure reads more semi-open than fully open world.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Original version of the anchor’s definitive edition; same core combat, exploration, progression, and story foundation with Proud Nordics adding expanded content.

Very close Falcom action RPG comp with fast real-time combat, traversal abilities, questing, boss fights, and story-led exploration, though its city-based structure differs from Ys X’s seafaring/island adventure.

Another highly compatible Ys game: lightning-fast action combat, exploration-heavy progression, frequent bosses, and authored adventure structure; party swapping and castaway-village loop make it close but not as direct as Ys IX or the base game.
Falcom action RPG with real-time combat, character switching, dungeon runs, bosses, and narrative progression, but it is more hub/social-segmented and less adventure-exploration oriented than Ys X.

Reasonable action-JRPG comp thanks to flashy party-based real-time combat, special team attacks, bosses, and story progression, but its combat feel and overall structure are broader and less Ys-like than the Falcom neighbors.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence strongly indicates a casual single-player merge/falling-block style puzzle game focused on dropping, stacking, and merging breads for score, with light customer-request interactions and leaderboard play. However, the available taxonomy archetypes do not include a general merge puzzle, falling-block puzzle, or score-attack puzzle category. The computed candidate of hidden_object_puzzle is not supported by the described gameplay loop, which centers on spatial dropping/merging rather than hidden-object searching. Because the game's identity is clear but does not cleanly fit any allowed archetype, it should be rejected rather than force-mapped.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Near-direct match: drop objects into a container, merge identical items into larger ones, manage physics and overflow, and play for score in short repeatable runs.
Very close Suika-like structure with item dropping, same-item merging, overflow fail state, and leaderboard/high-score focus; slightly less iconic than Suika Game as a benchmark.

Good arcade-puzzle neighbor through falling-piece spatial management, topping-out pressure, and replayable runs, but line clears and tetromino manipulation are a different core loop from physics-based merging.
